outputs.response_without_skill: The ownership wrapper is wrong. Because the view creates the `FeedViewModel`, it should not use `@StateObject` here. A plain `@State`-owned model is the right direction for this setup.
outputs.response_with_skill: `@StateObject` is the wrong ownership wrapper for an `@Observable` type. `@StateObject` belongs to the Combine `ObservableObject` world, so this view should own the model with `@State private var viewModel = FeedViewModel()`. Otherwise the Observation changes from `posts` are not tracked the right way and the view will not update as expected.

outputs.response_without_skill: `@ObservedObject` is not the right wrapper for this setup. Since the subview receives a model from its parent, either pass the model through more directly or keep the binding focused on the field you need rather than using the Combine-style wrapper.
outputs.response_with_skill: `@ObservedObject` is wrong here because `FormViewModel` is `@Observable`, not `ObservableObject`. Since this subview needs `$viewModel.name` for `TextField`, the correct wrapper is `@Bindable var viewModel: FormViewModel`. If the subview only needed read access, a plain `let viewModel` would be enough.

outputs.response_without_skill: There are a few issues here: the ViewModel should avoid UI-facing values like `Color`, and it should expose platform-neutral data instead. The type should also use the modern `@Observable final class` form rather than a loose base declaration.
outputs.response_with_skill: This ViewModel has three issues: it imports `SwiftUI` even though a ViewModel should stay platform-agnostic, it stores `Color` which should be exposed as a semantic value such as `headerColorName`, and it is missing the full `@Observable @MainActor final class` declaration so async state mutations are guaranteed to occur on the main actor. The corrected declaration is `@Observable @MainActor final class DashboardViewModel`, with only `Foundation` imported.

outputs.response_without_skill: `onAppear { Task { ... } }` is the problem. This is easy to duplicate on repeated appearances and is not the clean SwiftUI lifecycle hook for async loading. Prefer the `.task` modifier for initial loads.
outputs.response_with_skill: `onAppear { Task { ... } }` is the anti-pattern here. That task is unmanaged and is not cancelled automatically if the view disappears, and `onAppear` also runs every time the view reappears so users can trigger duplicate loads. Replace it with `.task { await viewModel.loadProducts() }`, which is lifecycle-aware and auto-cancelled by SwiftUI.

outputs.response_without_skill: The cancellation is getting surfaced to the user as a real error. When SwiftUI cancels in-flight work because the screen changes, that should not become an error banner. Ignore cancellation instead of mapping it into `.error`.
outputs.response_with_skill: The bug is that `CancellationError` gets caught by the generic `catch` and then surfaced to the user as an error state. In SwiftUI, `.task` work is cancelled when the view disappears or users switch tabs, so cancellation is expected and should be handled silently. Add `catch is CancellationError { return }` before the generic catch and only map real failures to `.error`.

outputs.response_without_skill: The view only loads once, so changing the filter never triggers a new request. Make the reload logic react to filter changes instead of only running on first appearance.
outputs.response_with_skill: The feed only loads once because `.task { await viewModel.loadFeed() }` runs on initial appearance but does not react to `selectedFilter` changes. Use `.task(id: viewModel.selectedFilter) { await viewModel.loadFeed() }` so SwiftUI cancels the previous task and launches a new one whenever the filter changes.

outputs.response_without_skill: The anti-pattern is fragmented state. Independent flags like `isLoading`, `errorMessage`, `articles`, and `isEmpty` can drift into contradictory combinations, so a single enum-based state would be cleaner.
outputs.response_with_skill: The anti-pattern is separate flags for loading, data, error, and emptiness. Those booleans allow impossible combinations like loading and error at the same time. Replace them with a single `ViewState<[Article]>` enum such as `.loading`, `.loaded([Article])`, `.empty`, and `.error(String)` so one tracked property replaces four inconsistent ones.

outputs.response_without_skill: This should render the enum more directly. Comparing and casting the state is brittle, and the view also fails to handle every possible state, so some outcomes render nothing to the user.
outputs.response_with_skill: This rendering should use pattern matching, not `==` comparisons and ad-hoc casting. Prefer an exhaustive `switch viewModel.state` or `if case` matching, then handle `.loading`, `.loaded`, `.empty`, and `.error` explicitly. As written, `.empty` and `.error` are missing, so the user just sees a blank screen for those states.

outputs.response_without_skill: This ViewModel is doing too much work directly and it also crashes on failures because of `try!`. Move to normal error handling with `do/catch`, and avoid doing raw request construction inline in the state-management code.
outputs.response_with_skill: There are three issues. The ViewModel performs a direct `URLSession` request instead of calling an injected repository protocol, it uses `try!` for both network and decoding so any failure crashes the app, and it needs explicit cancellation handling so task cancellation returns silently rather than poisoning state or crashing. The ViewModel should call something like `try await repository.fetchProducts()` inside a `do/catch`, map errors into `ViewState.error`, and handle `CancellationError` separately.

outputs.response_without_skill: String routes are fragile because they rely on ad-hoc string matching and are easy to mistype. A typed route model would make the navigation safer and easier to evolve.
outputs.response_with_skill: String-based routes are the anti-pattern. A typo like `product-detial` compiles and silently breaks navigation, and string routes are poor for state restoration. Define a typed `enum Route: Hashable` with cases such as `.productDetail(productID: UUID)` and switch over that in `.navigationDestination(for: Route.self)`.

outputs.response_without_skill: The navigation structure is overly nested and can produce odd toolbar and back-navigation behavior. Keep the tab navigation structure simpler so each tab owns its own navigation behavior.
outputs.response_with_skill: The outer `NavigationStack` wrapping the `TabView` is the violation. Each tab should own its own independent navigation stack, which the inner stacks already do. Keeping the outer stack causes duplicate toolbars, broken back-button behavior, and confused navigation state, so remove the outer wrapper and leave the per-tab `NavigationStack`s.

outputs.response_without_skill: The navigation concerns are spread across a few pieces of state in the ViewModel. That is a sign the app needs a more centralized routing approach or some intent callbacks instead of ad-hoc flags.
outputs.response_with_skill: Navigation state is in the wrong layer. Booleans like `showProfile` and `showSettings` plus `selectedItemID` should not live in the ViewModel. The ViewModel should signal intent through closures such as `onOpenProfile`, while the view owns sheet/navigation `@State`, or you can use a Router `@Observable` object holding a `NavigationPath` at the view layer. In either case, the ViewModel signals where to go and the View or Router decides how to present it.

outputs.response_without_skill: The ViewModel reaches into a singleton directly, which hides the dependency and hurts testability. Inject the service through the initializer so a mock can be supplied in tests.
outputs.response_with_skill: `APIService.shared` is a hidden singleton dependency, which makes the ViewModel hard to test because you cannot swap in a mock. Inject a protocol dependency through the initializer, for example `init(service: CartServiceProtocol)`, then pass the real service from the view environment and a mock in tests.

outputs.response_without_skill: For an `@Observable` theme service, use the newer environment-based injection approach rather than the older object-based pattern. The observation-native environment API is a better fit here.
outputs.response_with_skill: This injection is wrong for `@Observable`. `@EnvironmentObject` and `.environmentObject` are Combine APIs for `ObservableObject`, not the Observation-native pattern. Inject with `.environment(AppTheme())` and read it with `@Environment(AppTheme.self) var theme`; mixing the old wrappers with `@Observable` can crash at runtime or silently fail to observe updates.

outputs.response_without_skill: A shared network service should be registered centrally in the environment and read from views, then passed into ViewModels. That gives you one place to swap implementations, including in tests.
outputs.response_with_skill: For a shared service across many screens, define a custom environment entry and consume it from views. Use `@Entry` inside `EnvironmentValues` to declare something like `networkService`, override it at the root with `.environment(\.networkService, LiveNetworkService())`, and read it in a view with `@Environment(\.networkService)`. That same environment override pattern makes tests easy because you can inject `MockNetworkService()` without changing ViewModel code.

outputs.response_without_skill: The problem is actor isolation. The ViewModel is isolated to the main actor, so the test needs to run in that same context when it reads `sut.state`.
outputs.response_with_skill: The compiler is complaining because the ViewModel is `@MainActor`-isolated while the test class is not. Mark the entire test class `@MainActor` so property access such as `sut.state` happens on the same actor as the ViewModel. That is the standard fix for testing `@MainActor` SwiftUI ViewModels.

outputs.response_without_skill: You can test `load()` directly with `await` and assert on the resulting enum state, rather than reaching for expectations. The setup just needs the usual async-ready test structure around the SUT and mock repository.
outputs.response_with_skill: Two things are missing. First, add memory leak detection with `addTeardownBlock { [weak sut] in XCTAssertNil(sut) }` right after creating the SUT. Second, because both the test and the ViewModel are `@MainActor`, you can test `load()` with direct `await` and assertions rather than `XCTestExpectation`. After `await sut.load()`, use `if case .loaded(let products) = sut.state` to assert the loaded value.

outputs.response_without_skill: The test needs an async-capable `@MainActor` test context, a configurable mock repository, and assertions that verify cancellation does not produce an error state while also tracking how many times `search` was invoked.
outputs.response_with_skill: The full structure should use an `@MainActor` test class, a mock repository with `searchCallCount`, `stubbedResults`, and `shouldThrowCancellation`, plus leak detection through `addTeardownBlock { [weak sut] in XCTAssertNil(sut) }`. In the cancellation test, await `sut.search(query:)`, assert that the state did not become `.error`, and verify `mockRepo.searchCallCount == 1` so the behavior and the call tracking are both covered.

outputs.prompt: How do you diagnose excessive SwiftUI view redraws in an @Observable ViewModel?
outputs.response_without_skill: Use `Self._printChanges()` in the view body to see what redraws the view, then follow up with Instruments if you need more detail.
outputs.response_with_skill: Start with `Self._printChanges()` as the first diagnostic tool. Put it at the top of the view body to log every redraw and which tracked value triggered it, then remove it before committing. After that, use the SwiftUI Instruments template if you need quantitative redraw data.

outputs.response_without_skill: The likely architectural cause is that one large `HomeViewModel` is feeding both the feed and the search bar, so the view boundaries are too broad. Check for hidden reads first, and if needed split the responsibilities into smaller focused models.
outputs.response_with_skill: `@Observable` tracks the properties a view actually reads, so `FeedView` should only care about `posts` and `isRefreshing`, not `searchQuery`. First verify there are no hidden reads of `searchQuery` in the body or computed helpers. If redraws still fan out, the architectural fix is to split the large model into focused `FeedViewModel` and `SearchViewModel` types so typing in the search bar does not touch the feed's observation scope.

outputs.response_without_skill: The main problem is the giant app-wide ViewModel with too many unrelated properties. That broad surface area causes redraw fallout across features, so the model should be split into smaller feature-scoped pieces.
outputs.response_with_skill: There are two root causes. First, `AppViewModel` is a God ViewModel with too many unrelated properties, so changes fan out broadly and each feature sees a huge observation surface. Second, each view creates its own `@State private var vm = AppViewModel()`, so they are not sharing state at all. Split this into focused feature ViewModels and inject shared state from a common ancestor or via `@Environment` instead of constructing a fresh app-level model in every screen.

outputs.response_without_skill: The violation is using `@StateObject` with an `@Observable` model. The ownership wrapper should match the observation system, so this should be a state-owned model instead.
outputs.response_with_skill: This is a critical violation: `@StateObject` is being used with an `@Observable` class. `@StateObject` listens to Combine's `objectWillChange`, which `@Observable` does not provide, so the view stops updating when `count` changes. The fix is `@State private var viewModel = CounterViewModel()`.

outputs.response_without_skill: The most severe issue is the wrong ownership wrapper for the `@Observable` ViewModel. The lifecycle loading hook is also not ideal, and the separate loading and error flags should probably become a single state model.
outputs.response_with_skill: Ranked by severity: critical, `@StateObject` with an `@Observable` ViewModel breaks observation and must be replaced with `@State`; high, `onAppear { Task { ... } }` creates unmanaged repeated work and should become `.task`; medium, the separate `isLoading`/`cart`/`error` flags should become a single `ViewState<Cart>`. The button-triggered `Task` is acceptable for a short-lived user action, but `processPayment()` still needs proper cancellation handling internally.

outputs.response_without_skill: There are several serious issues here: the ViewModel mixes in platform concerns, the view ownership is unstable, and the loading lifecycle plus error handling are all heavier and riskier than they should be. This needs a more standard SwiftUI MVVM split.
outputs.response_with_skill: This audit has several layers. The critical issues are `SwiftUI` imported into the ViewModel, a `UIViewController` reference inside the ViewModel, and missing `@MainActor` isolation. The high-severity issues are the view owning the ViewModel as a plain property instead of `@State`, which recreates it on redraw, plus `onAppear { Task {} }` instead of `.task`. Then come the medium issues: `try!` network work, singleton access through `APIService.shared`, and separate boolean/error flags instead of a `ViewState` model.
